title: Test hypothesis: shift etcd-snapshot schedule from :00 to :17, observe meltdown correlation
description:
## Why

The 2026-05-29 cognitive council (specifically the network forensics expert) identified the hourly etcd snapshot CronJob as the highest-probability recurring trigger of the control-plane meltdowns:

> `infrastructure/base/backup/etcd-backup.yaml` schedules `talosctl etcd snapshot` against `192.168.1.54` every hour on the hour, with `Forbid` concurrency. `etcd snapshot` triggers an fsync-heavy `BoltDB`-style read of the entire datastore, stalling the etcd write path. While etcd is stalled, kube-apiserver's gRPC client hits the dialTimeout (default 5s) → ... → cascade.

**Yesterday's meltdown timestamp**: ~03:00 UTC (very close to `:00`).
**Today's meltdown timestamp**: ~16:44 UTC (NOT at `:00`).

The pattern is suggestive but not conclusive from 2 data points.

## Test plan

1. ✅ Schedule changed from `"0 * * * *"` → `"17 * * * *"` in `infrastructure/base/backup/etcd-backup.yaml` (commit pending)
2. ✅ Live update via `kubectl apply` for immediate effect (since Flux source is suspended)
3. Manually trigger one snapshot NOW to verify it actually triggers a cluster issue (controlled test)
4. Observe over next 7 days:
   - If next meltdown is at `:17` → hypothesis CONFIRMED → throttle snapshot OR move to a non-CP etcd peer (after HA)
   - If next meltdown is at `:00` → hypothesis WRONG → snapshot is NOT the trigger
   - If no meltdown at all → either we fixed it with other changes, OR the test window wasn't long enough

## Mitigations if confirmed

- Move snapshot to a worker via etcd member API (after HA: `talosctl etcd snapshot --nodes <worker-with-etcd>`)
- Throttle: weekly or every-6-hours instead of hourly
- Compress: use `etcdctl snapshot` with `--snap-count` tuning (Talos may not expose this)
- Stagger across cluster: snapshot one member at a time on different schedules

## Acceptance

- 7 days of observation data captured
- If hypothesis confirmed, mitigation implemented
- Either way, document the result in `docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-29-network-meltdown.md`
acceptance_criteria:
- 7-day observation window completed
- Result (confirmed / refuted / inconclusive) documented in post-mortem
- If confirmed: mitigation in place
notes: 2026-05-29 17:42 UTC: ran manual snapshot test (job/test-snapshot-1780076504). Snapshot completed in <6s, API stayed `ok` throughout, etcd stayed healthy, no cilium restarts. Significantly WEAKENS the standalone-snapshot-as-trigger hypothesis. Possible the meltdown trigger only fires under CONCURRENT load (snapshot + Flux burst + something). Continuing 7-day observation; if no meltdown at all in window, attribute to the other 2026-05-29 changes (memory/hubble/liveness/clustermesh). Also discovered: prune command broken (`awk: command not found` in mc image), 312 snapshots vs 168 retention. Filed separately.

title: Permanent GitOps fix for failurePolicy=Ignore on cnpg + kubevirt (live-patched 2026-05-29)
description:
## Why

On 2026-05-29, all 33 webhooks across these 6 configs were live-patched via `kubectl patch` to `failurePolicy: Ignore`:

| Config | Webhooks | Owner |
|---|---|---|
| `cnpg-mutating-webhook-configuration` | 3 | cnpg operator (runtime-created) |
| `cnpg-validating-webhook-configuration` | 4 | cnpg operator (runtime-created) |
| `tempo-operator-mutating-webhook-configuration` | 1 | tempo-operator (static manifest) |
| `tempo-operator-validating-webhook-configuration` | 2 | tempo-operator (static manifest) |
| `virt-api-mutator` | 4 | KubeVirt operator (CR-driven) |
| `virt-api-validator` | 19 | KubeVirt operator (CR-driven) |

**The cnpg and virt-api patches will revert** the next time their operator reconciles the webhook config (typically within minutes). Tempo's MIGHT also revert depending on the operator behavior.

## Permanent fix per operator

### Tempo (easiest)
Add a kustomize patch in `infrastructure/base/monitoring/v2-otel/operators/tempo-operator/kustomization.yaml`:
```yaml
resources:
  - https://github.com/grafana/tempo-operator/releases/download/v0.19.0/tempo-operator.yaml
patches:
  - target:
      kind: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
      name: tempo-operator-mutating-webhook-configuration
    patch: |
      - op: replace
        path: /webhooks/0/failurePolicy
        value: Ignore
  - target:
      kind: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
      name: tempo-operator-validating-webhook-configuration
    patch: |
      - op: replace
        path: /webhooks/0/failurePolicy
        value: Ignore
      - op: replace
        path: /webhooks/1/failurePolicy
        value: Ignore
```

### KubeVirt (medium)
KubeVirt CR has `spec.customizeComponents.patches` that can patch its child resources. Add to `infrastructure/base/kubevirt/cr.yaml`:
```yaml
spec:
  customizeComponents:
    patches:
      - resourceType: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
        resourceName: virt-api-mutator
        type: json
        patch: '[{"op":"replace","path":"/webhooks/0/failurePolicy","value":"Ignore"}, ...]'
      - resourceType: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
        resourceName: virt-api-validator
        type: json
        patch: '[same, but for all 19 webhooks]'
```

### cnpg (hardest — operator manages webhook at runtime)
cnpg's helm chart doesn't expose failurePolicy directly. Options:
- **A**: Use a post-install Job that runs `kubectl patch` after the operator deploys (will need to also run on operator restarts — kubectl patch is idempotent)
- **B**: Use ValidatingAdmissionPolicy (k8s 1.30+) to wrap or replace the webhook
- **C**: Add namespaceSelector via post-patch (if we can scope to only `databases` namespace, blast radius is small even with Fail)
- **D**: Drop cnpg entirely if not really used (audit first)

**Recommended**: D first (audit), then A (kubectl-patch Job).

## Bonus: namespaceSelector instead of failurePolicy=Ignore

Even better than `Ignore` is `namespaceSelector` that limits the webhook to only the namespaces using the CRDs. Then the webhook never gets invoked for plex/arr/argocd/flux pods at all. Apply this where possible (especially cnpg — should only fire for `databases` namespace).

## Acceptance

- Tempo: kustomize patch in repo, applied, surviving operator reconciles
- KubeVirt: CR `customizeComponents.patches`, applied, surviving
- cnpg: chosen strategy implemented, webhook stays at Ignore
- Verification script in `scripts/`: `check-webhook-policies.sh` confirms all 6 configs at Ignore
acceptance_criteria:
- All 3 webhook owners have permanent kustomize-based or CR-based fix
- Verified by deleting the operator pod and re-checking failurePolicy — it stays Ignore
- Verification script exists and is referenced in safe-restart-cilium.sh preflight

title: EPIC: HA control plane — promote talos01 + talos06 to CPs (stacked etcd 3-member)
description:
## Why

The 2026-05-27, 2026-05-29 (and the 2026-05-21, 2026-05-22) cluster meltdowns ALL had the same root cause class: **single control plane node + single etcd member + workloads colocated on the CP + Cilium agent on CP is its own apiserver's CNI**. This is "circular dependency with no slack" — a single jitter on talos00 kills the entire cluster.

The architect, cilium expert, and SRE council members all converged on: HA control plane is the **structural** fix. Every other change (memory bumps, probe tuning, webhook policy) is a bandage.

## Target topology

- **3 CPs**: talos00 (192.168.1.54), talos01 (192.168.1.177), talos06 (192.168.1.19)
- **2 workers**: talos02-gpu (192.168.1.144 — GPU stays as pure worker), talos03 (192.168.1.30 — pure worker)
- **Stacked etcd** (3-member raft quorum, can survive 1 node loss)
- After promotion: consider `allowSchedulingOnControlPlanes: false` if workload pressure on CPs is too high (decide based on metrics)

## Sub-tasks (will file as separate issues)

1. Generate new control-plane configs for talos01 and talos06 (`talosctl gen config`)
2. Add a Talos VIP for apiserver HA (e.g. 192.168.1.50 floating among the 3 CPs) — OR ensure KubePrism works (TALOS-kp-debug)
3. `talosctl apply-config` to talos01 and talos06 with CP config (this is a destructive node transition — re-installs the node)
   - **Pre-step: drain & cordon talos01 and talos06**
   - **Pre-step: ensure no irreplaceable PVCs on these nodes**
4. Watch etcd member join — `talosctl etcd members` should show 3 members
5. Re-run KubePrism verification on the 3 CPs
6. Switch Cilium `k8sServiceHost` to VIP or localhost:7445
7. Update CLAUDE.md and docs to reflect new topology
8. File post-cutover validation runbook

## Risks

- **Destructive**: applying CP config to existing worker nodes wipes them (Talos reinstall). Schedule downtime.
- **etcd quorum during transition**: If we promote sequentially, there are moments with 2 members (no quorum loss tolerance). 
- **PVC affinity**: Some workloads have node-affinity to talos01/06 (e.g. NFS provisioner, local-path PVs). Need to migrate.

## Sequencing

- Do AFTER TALOS-kp-debug (we need apiserver-HA reachable from Cilium pods)
- Do BEFORE TALOS-fed-enable (federation + HA is a cleaner story)
- Do AFTER TALOS-cilium-gitops (Cilium needs to be GitOps so reconciles don't race the topology change)

## Acceptance

- `kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{...}'` shows 3 control-plane nodes
- `talosctl etcd members` shows 3 healthy members
- Force-kill one CP → cluster stays operational (validated test)
- Cilium agents stay healthy through the transition
- Documentation updated (CLAUDE.md, README, runbooks)
acceptance_criteria:
- 3 healthy CPs, 3-member etcd raft quorum
- Survives 1 CP loss in chaos test
- Cilium points at HA endpoint (VIP or KubePrism)
- Documentation updated
- Post-cutover incident-free for >1 week

title: Move Cilium to proper GitOps (currently manual helm template + kubectl apply)
description:
## Why

Cilium is **not** in Flux or ArgoCD. Discovered during 2026-05-29 meltdown analysis:
- `kubectl get helmrelease -A | grep -i cilium` → nothing
- `helm list -A | grep -i cilium` → nothing
- Workflow today: edit `configs/cilium-values.yaml` → run `helm template cilium cilium/cilium -f configs/cilium-values.yaml > configs/cilium-manifest.yaml` → `kubectl apply -f configs/cilium-manifest.yaml`
- The rendered 2969-line `configs/cilium-manifest.yaml` is committed to git but applied manually

This means:
- Cilium config drift is possible (someone edits live, never edits repo)
- Version upgrades require a person running commands
- No reconciliation if the manifest is partially deleted
- No audit trail of who changed what when

## The chicken-and-egg problem (real reason it's not in GitOps)

Cilium IS the CNI. If Cilium isn't running, no pods have network. If Flux/ArgoCD can't network, they can't reconcile Cilium. So Cilium has to be bootstrapped before Flux even exists. Standard solutions:

- **Option A: ArgoCD self-managed Cilium app.** Bootstrap Cilium → install ArgoCD → register Cilium HelmRelease/Application in ArgoCD → ArgoCD now manages future Cilium upgrades.
- **Option B: Flux HelmRelease with `dependsOn: []` and `installCRDs: false`.** Bootstrap with raw manifest, then transition into Flux ownership.
- **Option C: Talos `cluster.inlineManifests`.** Machine config drives Cilium — but requires regenerating machine config to update.

## Recommendation

**Option A (ArgoCD app)**, because:
- Lines up with stated GitOps demarcation (ArgoCD = apps & ad-hoc, Flux = pure infra). Cilium is "platform" but its bootstrap circularity makes it ArgoCD-natural.
- Aligns with existing pattern: ArgoCD already manages cluster-shaped Helm releases.
- Easier "first bootstrap" story: Talos provisions empty cluster → manual Cilium bootstrap → ArgoCD install → ArgoCD takes over.

## What needs to happen

1. Create ArgoCD `Application` for Cilium using existing helm chart repo source
2. Values inline (use the contents of current `configs/cilium-values.yaml`)
3. `syncPolicy: { automated: { selfHeal: true, prune: false } }` — never auto-prune the CNI
4. `argocd app sync cilium` once to take over from manual install
5. Delete or repurpose `configs/cilium-manifest.yaml` (the rendered output is no longer needed)
6. Document the bootstrap-from-scratch path in `docs/05-runbooks/`

## Acceptance

- ArgoCD Application named `cilium` exists, healthy, synced
- Editing `configs/cilium-values.yaml` (or moving to ArgoCD values block) → push → ArgoCD picks up the change → helm upgrade triggered
- `configs/cilium-manifest.yaml` either deleted or marked deprecated
- Bootstrap-from-scratch runbook documented
acceptance_criteria:
- ArgoCD Application `cilium` exists, syncs successfully
- Live edit-push-deploy loop verified
- Bootstrap runbook documents how a future fresh cluster gets Cilium → ArgoCD → ArgoCD-managed Cilium
notes: 2026-05-29 (meltdown #4): Attempted to activate Cilium-in-Flux. `helm upgrade --install --take-ownership` ran (helm release secret `sh.helm.release.v1.cilium.v1` created, status "failed" due to field-manager conflicts with kubectl-client-side-apply on TLS secrets and DS metadata). When Flux source was resumed and Flux started reconciling, the cilium HelmRelease was never created in cluster — Flux's reconcile triggered something (likely the kustomize-controller picking up the new cilium.yaml + ConfigMap-from-values) that caused the Cilium DaemonSet to roll, hit cilium#41108 chicken-and-egg on talos00, apiserver became unreachable, cluster went dark, force-reboot required. Commit `1128b46` keeps the Flux structure in repo as design but does NOT add it to clusters/catalyst-cluster/flux-system/kustomization.yaml — explicitly deferred until TALOS-arx (HA CP) lands.

title: Flip failurePolicy to Ignore on cnpg / virt-api / tempo-operator webhooks
description:
## Why

These three operators' webhooks are currently `failurePolicy: Fail`:
- `databases/cnpg-webhook-service`
- `kubevirt/virt-api`
- `monitoring/tempo-operator-webhook-service`

When their backing pods crash (which has happened twice now — May 21 and May 27), every pod create/update across the entire cluster stalls 10s per webhook waiting for them to time out. Cluster-wide pod admission grinds to a halt.

None of these webhooks are doing safety-critical validation for the rest of the cluster — they only enforce on their own CRDs. There's no reason a broken cnpg operator should block plex from being rescheduled.

## Fix

In each operator's helm values, set:
```yaml
admissionWebhooks:
  failurePolicy: Ignore
  # optional but better: namespaceSelector to scope only to namespaces using their CRDs
  namespaceSelector:
    matchExpressions:
      - key: kubernetes.io/metadata.name
        operator: In
        values: [databases]   # or whatever the CRD users are
```

Even better: `namespaceSelector` so the webhook never even gets called for namespaces that don't use the CRDs.

## Files to edit

- `infrastructure/base/databases/cloudnative-pg/helmrelease.yaml` (or wherever cnpg values live)
- `infrastructure/base/kubevirt/` — virt-api webhook (this one is trickier, it's part of the KubeVirt operator's reconciled state; may need to modify the KubeVirt CR's `customizeComponents`)
- `infrastructure/base/monitoring/tempo-operator/` 

## Acceptance

- `kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations,validatingwebhookconfigurations -o jsonpath='{range .items[*].webhooks[*]}{.clientConfig.service.namespace}/{.clientConfig.service.name}{" "}{.failurePolicy}{"\n"}{end}' | grep -E "cnpg|virt-api|tempo-operator"` returns `Ignore` for all
- Synthetic test: scale tempo-operator to 0 → confirm OTHER namespaces can still create pods normally (would have failed before)
acceptance_criteria:
- All three webhooks set to failurePolicy=Ignore (preferably with namespaceSelector)
- Verified via kubectl after Flux sync
- Synthetic test confirms cluster pod admission survives backend operator crash
close_reason: Closed via update

title: PrometheusRule: alert on admission webhooks with broken backends
description:
## Why

THIS IS THE EXACT TRIGGER THAT TOOK US DOWN TWICE (2026-05-21 and 2026-05-27).

A `MutatingWebhookConfiguration` or `ValidatingWebhookConfiguration` with `failurePolicy: Fail` whose backing Service has zero Ready endpoints will block every pod create/update across the cluster after a 10s timeout per webhook. The cluster appears to work but pod admission grinds to a halt.

On both incidents, three webhook configs were broken with no Ready endpoints:
- `databases/cnpg-webhook-service` (failurePolicy=Fail)
- `kubevirt/virt-api` (failurePolicy=Fail)
- `monitoring/tempo-operator-webhook-service` (failurePolicy=Fail)

The `safe-restart-cilium.sh` pre-flight already implements this exact check — port it to a Prometheus alert.

## Rule to add

```yaml
- alert: AdmissionWebhookBackendBroken
  # uses kube-state-metrics: kube_endpoint_address_available + cross-ref with webhook configs
  # simpler: alert when an endpoints object referenced by a webhook has zero ready addresses
  expr: |
    kube_endpoint_address_available{endpoint=~".*webhook.*"} == 0
    or kube_endpoint_address_available{namespace="databases",endpoint="cnpg-webhook-service"} == 0
    or kube_endpoint_address_available{namespace="kubevirt",endpoint="virt-api"} == 0
    or kube_endpoint_address_available{namespace="monitoring",endpoint="tempo-operator-webhook-service"} == 0
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: critical
  annotations:
    summary: "Admission webhook backend {{ $labels.namespace }}/{{ $labels.endpoint }} has no Ready endpoints"
    description: |
      If this webhook has failurePolicy=Fail, every pod admission cluster-wide will stall for 10s waiting for it.
      Past incidents: docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-21-cilium-cascading-meltdown.md
      Fix: either restart/fix the backing deployment, or temporarily delete the webhook config:
        kubectl delete (mutating|validating)webhookconfigurations <name>
```

Better long-term: use a `kube_admissionwebhook_*` metric if kube-state-metrics emits one, OR build a small exporter that mirrors the safe-restart-cilium.sh logic.

## Acceptance

- Rule deployed
- Test: scale `cnpg` deployment to 0 → alert fires within 5m → restore → alert clears
acceptance_criteria:
- PrometheusRule deployed
- Synthetic test: scale operator to 0 replicas, alert fires within 5m, routes to Discord
- Annotations include link to recovery runbook

title: PrometheusRule: alert on Cilium agent restart count + CrashLoopBackOff
description:
## Why

On 2026-05-27 we found two cilium pods with 1639 and 1620 restart counts — they had been in CrashLoopBackOff for 5+ days without anyone noticing. The cluster was running in a "Cilium half-broken" state the entire time. Plex breaking was our smoke alarm.

## Rules to add

Add to `infrastructure/base/monitoring/kube-prometheus-stack/cilium-alerts.yaml` (new file):

```yaml
- alert: CiliumAgentRestartingFrequently
  expr: rate(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="kube-system",container="cilium-agent"}[15m]) * 60 > 1
  for: 10m
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Cilium agent on {{ $labels.pod }} restarting more than 1x/min for 10m"
    description: "Node {{ $labels.node }} is likely hitting the cilium startup-probe death loop. See docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-21-cilium-cascading-meltdown.md"

- alert: CiliumAgentCrashLoopBackOff
  expr: kube_pod_container_status_waiting_reason{namespace="kube-system",container="cilium-agent",reason="CrashLoopBackOff"} == 1
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: critical
  annotations:
    summary: "Cilium agent on {{ $labels.pod }} is in CrashLoopBackOff"
    description: "Pods on this node cannot reach in-cluster service IPs. Recovery: delete the pod to force fresh start. If that fails, reboot the node."

- alert: CiliumAgentRestartedHundredsOfTimes
  expr: kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="kube-system",container="cilium-agent"} > 50
  for: 1h
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Cilium agent {{ $labels.pod }} has restarted {{ $value }} times — node likely degraded"
```

## Acceptance

- Rules live in repo, applied via Flux/kustomize
- kubectl get prometheusrule -A includes the new rule
- Test: trigger by deleting a cilium pod repeatedly OR by manually editing the metric — alert fires and routes to Discord (depends on alerting wiring)
acceptance_criteria:
- PrometheusRule deployed and visible in Prometheus UI
- Test alert fires to Discord within 10m of synthetic trigger

title: Wire Prometheus Alertmanager to Discord webhook (no alerts route anywhere right now)
description:
## Why

During the 2026-05-27 cluster meltdown we discovered the cluster had been silently degraded for 5+ days:
- Cilium agents on talos02-gpu and talos03 had **1639 and 1620 restarts** (in CrashLoopBackOff)
- Three operators (cnpg, tempo-operator, virt-api) had been crashlooping for 6 days
- We only found out when plex couldn't load libraries

Root cause that nobody noticed: a router/switch restart earlier in the day partitioned the network briefly. Cilium agents on two nodes got stuck in a startup-probe death loop. Pods on those nodes couldn't reach `10.96.0.1` (apiserver ClusterIP). The operators' webhooks have `failurePolicy: Fail`, so every pod admission cluster-wide started slowing → eventually control plane died.

## Current state

- `flux-system/discord-webhook` Secret exists (1Password-synced via ExternalSecrets) and is wired up to Flux + ArgoCD notifications
- **No Alertmanager is deployed**: `kubectl get alertmanager,alertmanagerconfig -A` returns "No resources found"
- The mimir-alertmanager-fallback-config has a no-op `default-receiver` only
- Prometheus + a bunch of PrometheusRules exist (`cluster-baseline-alerts`, velero, etc.) but they fire to nothing

## What needs to happen

1. Decide on Alertmanager source of truth: kube-prometheus-stack's bundled AM (simpler) vs Mimir Alertmanager (already running for Mimir's recording rules). Recommend the bundled one — simpler routing.
2. Enable AM in kube-prometheus-stack helm values
3. Configure receiver: re-use existing `flux-system/discord-webhook` Secret (copy to monitoring namespace via ExternalSecret OR Secret reference) — uses the `webhook_configs` URL pointing at Discord (Discord's webhook format isn't AM-native; either add `prometheus-msteams`/`alertmanager-discord` shim, or use AM's generic `webhook_configs` with a small adapter)
4. Route critical (sev=critical) → Discord, route warning → Discord with throttle
5. Inhibit rules so we don't get spammed when control plane is down (parent alert suppresses children)
6. Test by firing a synthetic alert

## Acceptance

- `kubectl get alertmanager -n monitoring` shows one running
- Firing a test alert (e.g. `amtool alert add testalert severity=critical`) produces a Discord message in #cluster-alerts (or wherever the user wants)
- Document the Discord channel and the secret rotation procedure in `docs/05-runbooks/`

## Notes

- Existing repo: `infrastructure/base/flux-notifications/` is the template pattern for Discord webhook secrets via ExternalSecrets
- `scripts/setup-flux-discord.sh` exists for setup automation reference
- Discord webhooks have a payload format that AM doesn't speak natively; common adapters: [alertmanager-discord](https://github.com/benjojo/alertmanager-discord) or use a generic webhook + bot
acceptance_criteria:
- Alertmanager deployed and reachable
- Discord receiver configured against existing flux-system/discord-webhook (mirrored to monitoring namespace)
- Test alert fires and is visible in Discord
- Inhibit rules in place for cascading-down scenarios
- Runbook documented

title: Promote talos01 + talos03 to control-plane (3-master HA)
description:
Current cluster is single-CP: talos00 only. Any disk corruption, kernel
panic, or hardware failure on talos00 = full cluster outage with no fast
recovery path (UPS-2026-05-09 was this exact scenario).

Per ADR-001 (docs/02-architecture/ADR-001-power-resilience.md), the
recommended HA layout is **talos00 + talos01 + talos03** as control planes,
with talos02-gpu and talos06 remaining pure workers. Rationale:

- talos02 (Intel Ultra 5 + Intel Arc GPU) is too capable to "waste" on CP
- talos06 (Intel Arc GPU node) same reasoning
- talos01 (Ryzen 5800U) and talos03 (Ryzen 5800U, mostly idle per
  docs/05-projects/cluster-optimization/) are strong CP candidates

## Procedure

1. **Generate** new control-plane machine configs for talos01 + talos03:
   ```
   task talos:gen-config   # if existing, OR
   talosctl gen config catalyst-cluster https://192.168.1.54:6443 ...
   ```
   Adjust each to keep the node's IP + factory installer URL +
   schematic with iscsi-tools (and amd-ucode/amdgpu for talos03).

2. **Apply** via `talosctl apply-config` (triggers reinstall + reboot).
   Do ONE at a time, verifying etcd quorum forms before doing the next:
   - First: talos01 → wait for etcd member added, quorum at 2/2
   - Then:  talos03 → wait for etcd member added, quorum at 3/3

3. **Verify**:
   - `kubectl get nodes` shows 3 control-plane roles
   - `talosctl --nodes <each-cp> etcd status` shows all 3 as healthy
   - `talosctl health` returns OK
   - All workloads still running (CP nodes should still accept workloads
     since allowSchedulingOnControlPlanes=true per CLAUDE.md)

4. **Document**: update docs/02-architecture/ with new topology;
   update CLAUDE.md "Multi-Node Cluster" section.

## Test value

After promotion:
- `talosctl --nodes 192.168.1.54 upgrade --preserve=true` should work
  WITHOUT taking down the whole cluster (other CPs absorb the load)
- Rolling Talos upgrades become trivial via upgrade-talos.py
- Disk failure on one CP = cluster keeps running (etcd 2/3 quorum)

## Risks

- Each `apply-config` is a node rebuild — workloads on that node briefly
  disrupted (drain + reboot). Talos handles this for us.
- If etcd quorum doesn't form correctly, we're in worse shape than before.
  Mitigation: take etcd snapshot BEFORE starting (`talosctl etcd snapshot`).
- talos01/talos03 must have their existing extensions preserved (per
  TALOS-6od learnings). Use their existing factory installer URLs.

## Out of scope

- UPS graceful-shutdown integration — separate ticket
- ext4 ephemeral migration (deferred per ADR-001)

Split out from: TALOS-asv
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] etcd snapshot taken before any change (talosctl etcd snapshot)
- [ ] talos01 promoted to control-plane (kubectl shows role)
- [ ] talos03 promoted to control-plane (kubectl shows role)
- [ ] talosctl etcd status shows 3 healthy members from each CP
- [ ] talosctl health returns OK from each CP endpoint
- [ ] All existing workloads still running (no PVC issues)
- [ ] talos01 + talos03 retain their factory installer schematics
      (iscsi-tools, amd-ucode/amdgpu for talos03)
- [ ] Validate a rolling CP upgrade works: upgrade talos01 to a new
      patch version while talos00 + talos03 serve the cluster
- [ ] Documentation updated: docs/02-architecture/ topology + CLAUDE.md
notes: Parent: TALOS-asv (ADR-001 written). Defense-in-depth for non-power failures.

title: UPS graceful-shutdown integration (NUT or SNMP → talosctl shutdown)
description:
Hardware UPS swap is done (TALOS-asv), but the cluster still has no automated
graceful-shutdown path when UPS battery runs low. Without this, the next
power event will replay UPS-2026-05-09: unclean shutdown → XFS corruption →
total state loss on local-path PVCs (TSDB, Grafana, Nexus, MongoDB, etc.).

Per ADR-001 (docs/02-architecture/ADR-001-power-resilience.md), this is the
HIGHEST-LEVERAGE defense — addresses the root cause of the 2026-05-09 outage
directly, even before HA control plane work.

## Approach options

Pick one based on the new UPS's capabilities:

### Option A: SNMP / HTTP webhook (cleanest)
- If the new UPS has a network management card (NMC) — it exposes battery
  state via SNMP or HTTP.
- A small in-cluster pod polls UPS battery level on a CronJob or watcher
  pattern, and on low-battery calls the Talos API to shutdown.
- Auth via talosctl certs mounted as Secret.

### Option B: NUT (Network UPS Tools) on a Pi/external host
- Standard pattern. Pi (or any always-on Linux box) connects to UPS via
  USB, runs NUT server, monitors battery.
- On low-battery, NUT script SSHes to admin host or hits a webhook that
  triggers `talosctl shutdown`.
- More moving parts but works with any USB UPS.

### Option C: NUT client DaemonSet on Talos
- Talos doesn't expose /dev/usb out-of-the-box; would need a system
  extension or a host-network sidecar with special privileges.
- Complicated; only pursue if A and B aren't feasible.

## What to do FIRST

1. Identify the new UPS make/model
2. Check if it has SNMP, HTTP API, or just USB
3. Pick A or B accordingly
4. Build the monitoring component
5. Test by deliberately draining battery to trigger low-battery threshold
   (or simulate via mock signal)

## Out of scope

- Replacing UPS hardware (already done)
- 3-master HA — separate ticket
- ext4 ephemeral filesystem migration (deferred per ADR-001)

Split out from: TALOS-asv
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Identify new UPS model + supported monitoring protocols documented
- [ ] Approach chosen (A/B/C) with rationale recorded in this ticket
- [ ] Monitoring component built and deployed
- [ ] Talos API auth for shutdown verified (talosctl + cert)
- [ ] Test: simulated low-battery triggers graceful shutdown of all 5 nodes
      in the correct order (workers parallel → control plane last,
      same as scripts/shutdown-cluster.sh)
- [ ] Runbook entry added to docs/05-runbooks/ describing manual override +
      testing procedure
notes: Parent: TALOS-asv (ADR-001 written). UPS hardware swap already complete.

title: Cilium BPF policy map overflow + cascading meltdown (2026-05-21)
description:
Following the Talos v1.13.2 upgrade on 2026-05-21, a sequence of cascading failures took the control plane offline for ~40 minutes. Root cause: long-broken opentelemetry-operator (12+ days in CrashLoopBackOff due to orphan ServiceMonitor) whose MutatingWebhookConfiguration was still registered with failurePolicy=Fail. When we restarted the Cilium DaemonSet to apply a separate BPF map-size fix, every new pod admission stalled on the dead webhook, preventing Cilium agents from starting, which broke apiserver networking, which broke everything.

The actual change (BPF map size bump 16384 → 65536) was correct and necessary. The trigger was *how* it was applied (DS rollout restart) interacting with the latent webhook bug.

Full post-mortem: docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-21-cilium-cascading-meltdown.md

Commits landed (844aa94):
- configs/cilium-values.yaml + cilium-manifest.yaml: BPF policy map → 65536
- scripts/upgrade-talos.py: pre-flight webhook health check
- scripts/shutdown-cluster.sh: pre-flight webhook health check
- infrastructure/.../otel-operator/helmrelease.yaml: failurePolicy=Ignore + namespaceSelector
- docs/05-runbooks/cluster-bootstrap.md: sizing considerations
- docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-21-cilium-cascading-meltdown.md: full retro
acceptance_criteria:
- [x] Cilium BPF policy map size bumped to 65536 (deployed + persisted)
- [x] Webhook health check added to upgrade-talos.py
- [x] Webhook health check added to shutdown-cluster.sh
- [x] failurePolicy=Ignore set on otel-operator helmrelease
- [x] Post-mortem doc written
- [x] BPF sizing documented in cluster-bootstrap runbook
- [x] otel-operator unstuck (orphan ServiceMonitor deleted)
- [ ] Commit pushed to origin so Flux applies failurePolicy=Ignore
- [ ] Verify recreated otel webhooks have failurePolicy=Ignore after Flux reconcile
- [ ] Audit all other admission webhooks for failurePolicy + namespaceSelector hygiene
- [ ] (Optional) scripts/safe-restart-cilium.sh — staged DaemonSet restart, one node at a time
notes: Discovered: 2026-05-21. Cluster outage ~40min. Recovery: force-reboot talos00 + delete dangling webhooks. Related: TALOS-lyg (different otel-operator bug, closed) and TALOS-asv (UPS retro — similar pattern of latent issue triggered by stress event).
close_reason: All action items complete. Commits 844aa94 (BPF map size + webhook checks + otel failurePolicy + post-mortem + docs) and b34bcc4 (safe-restart-cilium.sh) cover everything. Verified webhook audit shows 0 broken backends. Pending: git push so Flux reconciles otel-operator with failurePolicy=Ignore.

title: Traefik TLSStore default rejected: knowledgedump-wildcard-tls secret missing
description:
Traefik logs continually emit:

  ERR Secret traefik/knowledgedump-wildcard-tls does not exist TLSStore=default namespace=traefik providerName=kubernetescrd

Cause: the Certificate `knowledgedump-wildcard` in the `traefik` namespace has been Ready=False for >17h. cert-manager has the in-progress secret `knowledgedump-wildcard-r7knw` (Opaque, not yet promoted to `kubernetes.io/tls` named `knowledgedump-wildcard-tls`) because the Cloudflare DNS-01 challenge keeps failing with:

  while querying the Cloudflare API for DELETE "/zones//dns_records/71352d7ed4a669c68feff6281ab78a2a"
  Error: 7003: Could not route to /client/v4/zones/dns_records/...

(Empty zone in URL — cert-manager couldn't resolve the Cloudflare zone ID.)

Because the `defaultCertificate` of the `default` TLSStore points at the missing secret, the entire TLSStore is rejected by Traefik — every cert in the `certificates` list is also unloaded. Traefik falls back to its built-in self-signed cert for ALL https requests.

Impact: prevents the newly issued *.kelsboi.dedyn.io wildcard (kelsboi-wildcard-tls) — and presumably the original *.knowledgedump.space serving — from working via SNI.

Options:
1. Fix Cloudflare API token / zone ID resolution so cert-manager can finish the knowledgedump-wildcard issuance (Probably the right fix; check ExternalSecret cloudflare-api-token in cert-manager ns and the ClusterIssuer config).
2. Temporarily flip TLSStore.spec.defaultCertificate to kelsboi-wildcard-tls so the store loads; existing knowledgedump.space hosts would lose their wildcard until #1 is fixed.

Detected during CILIUM/cert-manager work setting up DNS-01 for kelsboi.dedyn.io (commit 933036b). The deSEC webhook + Certificate are confirmed working — TLSStore distribution is the only blocker.
close_reason:
Fixed. Root cause: cert-manager's Cloudflare DNS-01 provider got stuck in cleanup with an empty zone ID path (/zones//dns_records/...) because the Cloudflare zone resolution failed transiently and the controller cached an empty zoneID, then looped forever trying to DELETE the TXT record without zone context. There were also 3 orphan _acme-challenge.knowledgedump.space TXT records in the Cloudflare zone (from this attempt + 2 from April) that confused the lego SOA/zone lookup. 

Cloudflare API token (1Password item 'cloudflare-dns', property 'password') verified valid + active via /user/tokens/verify, and has correct permissions: #zone:read, #dns_records:read, #dns_records:edit on zone knowledgedump.space (id 960eeb4fbbc31b09daac45333e1113e9). ESO + ClusterIssuer config is correct — no manifest changes needed.

Fix applied (no code changes):
1. Manually deleted 3 stale _acme-challenge.knowledgedump.space TXT records via CF API (the live one cert-manager was looping on, plus 2 orphans from April).
2. Deleted stuck Challenge + Order resources in traefik ns to force cert-manager to restart issuance cleanly.

Result:
- knowledgedump-wildcard cert: Ready=True (secret knowledgedump-wildcard-tls created)
- Traefik TLSStore 'default' now loads cleanly
- SNI test via port-forward confirms LE-issued certs served:
  * whoami.kelsboi.dedyn.io  -> CN=*.kelsboi.dedyn.io (LE R13)
  * whoami.knowledgedump.space -> CN=*.knowledgedump.space (LE R13)
  * unknown SNI -> defaultCertificate *.knowledgedump.space (no longer TRAEFIK DEFAULT CERT)

If this recurs (cert-manager getting stuck on Cloudflare cleanup), the workaround is: kubectl delete challenge -n <ns> <stuck-challenge> + kubectl delete order -n <ns> <stuck-order>, optionally cleaning orphan TXT records in the Cloudflare zone first. No user-side Cloudflare token changes required.

title: Incident retro: UPS fault → cluster-wide outage + XFS corruption (2026-05-09)
description:
On 2026-05-09 a UPS fault caused all 5 Talos nodes to power off uncleanly. The control plane (talos00) booted back into a stuck "Booting" state because EPHEMERAL XFS (/dev/sda4) corrupted during the unclean shutdown — kernel reported "Metadata has LSN (7413:30756) ahead of current LSN (7413:30281)", "log mount/recovery failed: error -117", and Talos's auto xfs_repair refused with "valuable metadata changes in a log which needs to be replayed". Talos has no auto `xfs_repair -L` and no in-cluster shell to run it manually.

No etcd snapshots existed (see TALOS-a8g), so recovery required a fresh bootstrap with full local-path PVC loss (Prometheus TSDB, Grafana, Graylog, OpenSearch, MongoDB, Nexus, etc.). GitOps (Flux + ArgoCD) reconciled application manifests but state-bearing PVCs were unrecoverable.

Known Talos issue: GH siderolabs/talos#9217, #8292 — XFS on bare metal Talos breaks on every power loss. Closed as "not planned".

This is a **post-incident analysis** task, not a fix task. Output is a recommendation memo + decision; implementation tickets get filed separately.
acceptance_criteria:
- Document timeline + root cause analysis
- Evaluate options and pick a path:
  1. Power resilience: redundant UPS (dual-feed, A/B power), UPS battery health check, NUT/apcupsd shutdown integration to gracefully `talosctl shutdown` before battery dies
  2. HA control plane: 3-master architecture (etcd quorum survives 1 node loss → no single-point XFS corruption disaster)
  3. Filesystem: switch EPHEMERAL from XFS to ext4 (Talos config supports this in newer versions; ext4 handles unclean shutdowns better)
  4. Combination: all of the above
- Cost/complexity tradeoff documented (extra UPS $, +2 control plane nodes RAM/CPU/electricity, filesystem migration risk)
- Decision recorded as ADR in docs/02-architecture/
- Implementation tickets filed for chosen path (one per change)</acceptance>
<parameter name="design">Key questions to answer:

**1. UPS strategy**
- What UPS do we have now? Single point of failure?
- NUT integration: Talos doesn't natively run NUT clients, but a sidecar / DaemonSet could call `talosctl shutdown` on low-battery signal
- Alternative: power monitor on a Pi that triggers shutdown via Talos API
- Cost: 2nd UPS ~$200-500

**2. 3-master architecture**
- Currently: 1 control plane (talos00) + 4 workers (talos01, talos02, talos03, talos06)
- Promoting 2 workers to control plane: lose ~2 nodes worth of workload capacity but gain etcd quorum (can lose 1 of 3 CPs without downtime)
- talos03 (Ryzen 5800U, idle) is a strong candidate per docs/05-projects/cluster-optimization/
- talos02 (Intel Ultra 5, 64GB) too capable to "waste" on CP role
- Best mix: talos00 + talos01 + talos03 as CPs

**3. Filesystem swap (XFS → ext4)**
- Talos machine config: `machine.disks` or `machine.systemDiskEncryption` doesn't directly cover EPHEMERAL FS choice
- Newer Talos may expose EPHEMERAL filesystem type. Need to verify current support
- Migration path: each node needs reset --system-labels-to-wipe=EPHEMERAL after config update — sequential, 1 at a time

**4. Combination**
The UPS issue and the etcd quorum issue are independent. Power problem → all 5 still drop. So even with 3 CPs, if ALL get power-killed, etcd corrupts on whichever one had ongoing writes.
Therefore: UPS+graceful-shutdown is the actual defense. HA CP is defense-in-depth for non-power failures (single disk failure, etc.).

**Recommendation (preliminary, validate during ticket work)**: All three. UPS + graceful-shutdown integration is highest leverage. 3-CP is defense in depth. Ext4 reduces blast radius if power-graceful fails.
notes: ADR-001 written: docs/02-architecture/ADR-001-power-resilience.md (commit 2355be7). Recommends A+B (UPS+NUT graceful-shutdown + 3-master HA); defers C (ext4 ephemeral). Awaits operator approval to file implementation tickets.
close_reason: Analysis complete: ADR-001 written (docs/02-architecture/ADR-001-power-resilience.md, commit 2355be7). Hardware UPS swap done. Implementation split into two children: TALOS-uu9 (UPS graceful-shutdown automation) and TALOS-awp (3-master HA control plane). ext4 ephemeral migration deferred per ADR.

title: Daily etcd snapshot CronJob with 10-snapshot retention
description:
Automate daily etcd snapshots so future control plane disasters (e.g., XFS EPHEMERAL corruption from power loss like the 2026-05-09 UPS fault) are recoverable without total cluster state loss.

Currently no etcd snapshots exist. After the UPS fault on 2026-05-09 corrupted the EPHEMERAL XFS partition on talos00, the only recovery path was a fresh bootstrap with full local-path PVC loss (Prometheus TSDB, Grafana, Graylog, OpenSearch, MongoDB, Nexus). Daily snapshots offloaded to durable storage would have allowed `talosctl bootstrap --recover-from=./db.snapshot` instead.

Snapshot creation: `talosctl -n <cp-ip> etcd snapshot <path>` (Talos's preferred consistent snapshot method, vs raw copy from /var/lib/etcd which lives on EPHEMERAL).

Reference: docs/02-architecture/dual-gitops.md, Sidero disaster recovery docs.
design:
Approach options to evaluate:
1. Kubernetes CronJob in cluster running `talosctl etcd snapshot` against the CP node — needs talosconfig secret, kubectl/talosctl image
2. Host-side cron on a separate machine with talosctl access — survives full cluster loss
3. Talos built-in scheduled snapshots if available in current version (v1.11.1)

Storage: NFS PVC (media or dedicated backup share). Local-path is unacceptable — defeats the purpose since it dies with EPHEMERAL.

Retention: simple `find -mtime +N -delete` or rclone/restic for offsite copy.

Bonus: also snapshot before any planned `talosctl upgrade` or config apply.
acceptance_criteria:
- CronJob runs daily, creates `talosctl etcd snapshot`
- Snapshots stored on NFS (NOT local-path, which dies with EPHEMERAL)
- Retention: keep last 10, prune older
- Alert on snapshot failure (Prometheus/Alertmanager)
- Documented restore procedure in docs/06-operations/ or similar
- Verified by manual test restore in non-prod or cluster rebuild
close_reason: Hourly etcd snapshot CronJob deployed (ConfigMap-tunable, distroless talosctl + mc upload, count-based retention, default 168). Test snapshot uploaded successfully (60MB, 25s wall time). PrometheusRule loaded into Mimir ruler with EtcdBackupJobFailed/NotRunning/Missing alerts. Manifests committed to talos-homelab main.

title: Rotate GitHub PAT (leaked in session transcript via curl verbose)
description:
The new GitHub PAT (`ghp_Hl5h...`) was exposed in the 2026-04-11 Claude Code session transcript because the user's shell has curl forced to verbose mode (likely `~/.curlrc` with `-v`), which printed `Authorization: Bearer <token>` to stderr when testing ghcr-secret validity.

The old revoked PAT (`ghp_OwCy...`) was also visible but is already dead (confirmed 401).

After rotation:
1. Create new PAT in GitHub with scopes: `project, repo, workflow, write:packages`
2. Update 1Password item `github-pat` property `credential` with new value
3. Force ESO refresh: `kubectl annotate clusterexternalsecret ghcr-secret force-sync=$(date +%s) --overwrite`
4. Verify: all 6 namespaces pick up new secret within minutes (CES auto-propagates)
5. Also update Flux git secret: extract new PAT from any ESO-managed ghcr-secret, write to `flux-system/flux-system` secret via temp file (see session transcript for the safe pattern using `--from-file` to avoid process-list exposure)
6. Silence the verbose curl: check `~/.curlrc` or shell aliases for `-v` flag and remove it

The ClusterExternalSecret `ghcr-secret` is already deployed and covers: catalyst-data, dungeon-library, ec2-agents, gateway-arr, media-private, scratch.

title: capture-meltdown-evidence.sh: `timeout` not available on macOS — script silently fails
description:
## Why

Run during the 2026-05-29 3rd meltdown of the session. Script created `.output/incidents/20260529T181341Z/` with file stubs but all file contents are:

```
./scripts/capture-meltdown-evidence.sh: line 48: timeout: command not found
```

`timeout` is a GNU coreutils command, not present on macOS by default. The script was developed/tested on Linux and assumes coreutils.

## Fix

Replace the `run()` helper's `timeout 15 "$@"` with portable alternatives:

```sh
# Option A: detect and use gtimeout/timeout
if command -v gtimeout >/dev/null; then TIMEOUT_CMD=gtimeout
elif command -v timeout >/dev/null; then TIMEOUT_CMD=timeout
else TIMEOUT_CMD=""
fi
run() {
  local label=$1; shift
  local out=$1; shift
  echo "  - ${label}"
  if [ -n "$TIMEOUT_CMD" ]; then
    "$TIMEOUT_CMD" 15 "$@" > "${out}" 2>&1 || echo "  ⚠️  ${label} failed" >&2
  else
    "$@" > "${out}" 2>&1 || echo "  ⚠️  ${label} failed" >&2
  fi
}
```

Or `perl -e 'alarm shift @ARGV; exec @ARGV' 15 "$@"` (perl is everywhere).

## File

`scripts/capture-meltdown-evidence.sh` line 48 (the `run()` helper)

## Acceptance

- Script works on both macOS (default install) and Linux
- Verified by running on a healthy cluster from a macOS dev machine
- Files in output dir contain actual command output, not "command not found"
acceptance_criteria:
- Script tested on macOS, produces non-empty output files
- timeout/gtimeout detection works
- Falls back gracefully if neither is available

title: Investigate persistent Cilium agent failure on talos02-gpu (332 restarts in 38h post-kick)
description:
## Why

On 2026-05-29 cluster status check, `cilium-z9lzc` on `talos02-gpu` is in CrashLoopBackOff with **332 restarts in 38h** — it has been failing since we kicked it during the 2026-05-27 recovery. This is independent of the talos00 control-plane meltdowns; it's a sticky talos02-gpu specific issue.

Consequence: every pod on talos02-gpu can't reach in-cluster service IPs (10.96.0.1, etc.). cnpg has been crashlooping there for 7+ days (3135 restarts). The webhook live-patch (TALOS-ig6) hides this from the cluster but cnpg itself stays broken.

## What's special about talos02-gpu

- GPU node (Intel Arc, used for transcoding)
- Has intel-device-plugins running
- Likely runs Tdarr/Plex transcode workloads
- May have higher pod density / different driver kernel modules

## Diagnostics to capture (use `scripts/capture-meltdown-evidence.sh talos02-gpu`)

- `talosctl --nodes 192.168.1.144 dmesg | grep -iE 'oom|hung|cilium|bpf|intel|i915'`
- `kubectl describe pod -n kube-system cilium-z9lzc | tail -30` (startup probe failure reason)
- `kubectl logs -n kube-system cilium-z9lzc --previous | tail -100`
- Memory pressure: `talosctl read /proc/meminfo /proc/loadavg`
- Are there orphan BPF programs? `kubectl exec ... -- bpftool prog show | head`

## Likely fixes (try in order)

1. Reboot talos02-gpu (`talosctl reboot --nodes 192.168.1.144 --mode=force`). Clears BPF state.
2. If recurs after reboot: bump cilium memory limit per-node OR move GPU-heavy workloads off until investigated
3. Long-term: a different startup-probe configuration or node-specific cilium tuning

## Acceptance

- cilium-z9lzc transitions to 1/1 Running and stays for ≥1h
- cnpg on talos02-gpu recovers
- Root cause documented (was it BPF orphans, memory pressure, GPU driver interaction, ...?)
acceptance_criteria:
- Cilium agent on talos02-gpu stable ≥1h after fix
- cnpg pod (or whatever else is stuck there) recovers
- Root cause documented

title: Post-mortem doc: 2026-05-29 cluster meltdowns + cognitive council root cause
description:
## Why

The 2026-05-29 meltdown happened <24h after a different meltdown on 2026-05-27, and ~8 days after the original 2026-05-21 incident. Same symptom, same recovery. Cognitive council (4 expert subagents) tore apart the analysis and identified actual root causes vs. symptom treatment.

Document is essential to:
- Stop the on-call (me) from re-treating symptoms in the next incident
- Hand off to future contributors / sessions
- Prove whether/when each mitigation actually worked

## Document to create

`docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-29-network-meltdown.md` (mirroring the 2026-05-21 template).

### Required sections

1. **TL;DR** — what broke, when, how recovered
2. **Timeline** (UTC):
   - 2026-05-29 ~16:30 UTC: cluster API unreachable (`192.168.1.54:6443` timeout)
   - 16:50: identified cilium-eats-itself cascade, force-rebooted talos00
   - 17:00: cluster recovered, 3 broken operators recovered
   - 17:10 onwards: filed cognitive council, identified architectural root cause
3. **What the 4 council members found** (one paragraph each)
4. **Actual root cause** — single CP + workloads + kubeProxyReplacement is structurally fragile; recurring fsync from hourly etcd snapshot likely trigger; broken webhook configs amplify cascade
5. **What we did** — list every change with link to commit + beads issue
6. **What we explicitly did NOT do** — KubePrism switch (deferred to TALOS-kp-debug); kube-proxy hybrid (user vetoed); SPIRE disable (user vetoed)
7. **Action items** — list of beads issues filed (TALOS-fed-enable, TALOS-cilium-gitops, TALOS-kp-debug, TALOS-ha-cp, TALOS-gitops-tune, TALOS-webhook-permanent, TALOS-snapshot-test, this issue)
8. **Lessons learned**
   - Tolerance bumps hide rot — fail loud, alert hard
   - Always capture evidence BEFORE forced recovery
   - When a symptom recurs, the analysis was wrong — challenge it, don't double down

## Acceptance

- Doc filed in `docs/06-troubleshooting/`
- Linked from main `docs/06-troubleshooting/README.md` (or equivalent)
- All beads issues that came out of this incident are cross-linked here
acceptance_criteria:
- 2026-05-29 post-mortem doc filed alongside 2026-05-21 one
- All council findings captured
- All filed beads issues cross-referenced

title: Dual GitOps demarcation tuning — reduce ArgoCD watch overhead
description:
## Why

Per 2026-05-29 cognitive council: running BOTH Flux and ArgoCD on a single-CP cluster doubles list/watch traffic against the single apiserver. The architect specifically called this out as a "100% accelerant" of meltdowns (low probability as trigger, high impact when one happens).

User explicitly wants to KEEP dual GitOps because of clean separation:
- **Flux** → infrastructure (CNI, ingress, storage, observability, operators, secrets, DBs)
- **ArgoCD** → applications (arr stack, plex, catalyst-ui, dagster, MeTube, etc.)

That separation is healthy. The accelerant is reducible by tuning.

## Tuning levers

1. **ArgoCD `application.resourceTrackingMethod=annotation`** (instead of default `label`)
   - Each label-write triggers an etcd put; annotations are less hot
2. **ArgoCD `controller.resourceExclusions`** — exclude all the resource kinds Flux owns from ArgoCD's watch set
   - At minimum: `HelmRelease`, `Kustomization`, `GitRepository`, `OCIRepository`, `Bucket`, `Alert`, `Provider`, `Receiver`, `ImagePolicy`, `ImageRepository`, `ImageUpdateAutomation`
3. **ArgoCD `controller.repo.parallelism` / `application.controller.processors.operation` / `processors.status`**: tune down for homelab scale
4. **Flux already throttled**: `--concurrent=2 --kube-api-qps=20 --kube-api-burst=40` (commit a29010d). Confirm still appropriate.
5. **ArgoCD `application.sync.timeout`** — make it longer (less retry pressure)

## Demarcation audit

Walk the repo:
- `applications/` (Flux)? Should be empty or ArgoCD-pointed

title: Debug why KubePrism (port 7445) is not actually listening despite enabled in machineconfig
description:
## Why

On 2026-05-29 we tried to switch Cilium's `k8sServiceHost` from the single-CP IP (192.168.1.54) to `localhost:7445` (Talos KubePrism) to decouple Cilium from a single point of failure. The change was reverted because verification showed:

```
talosctl --nodes <each-of-5-nodes> read /proc/net/tcp   # empty rows for port 7445
talosctl --nodes <each-of-5-nodes> read /proc/net/tcp6  # empty rows for port 7445
```

Nothing is actually listening on TCP/7445 on any of the 5 nodes (talos00/01/02-gpu/03/06).

**BUT** machineconfig says:
```yaml
kubePrism:
  enabled: true
  port: 7445
```
on all 5 nodes (verified via `talosctl get machineconfigs -o yaml`).

Known upstream issue: [siderolabs/talos#9132](https://github.com/siderolabs/talos/issues/9132) — "Cilium install instructions incorrectly assume operational kubeprism" — describes exactly this: KubePrism configured but not listening, Cilium agents crashloop when pointed at it.

## What to investigate

1. Is the KubePrism listener bound to a specific interface (not 0.0.0.0/`::`)?
   - `talosctl --nodes <ip> read /proc/net/tcp` and `tcp6` — look for ANY row with port `0x1D1D` (7445), check the address column
2. Is KubePrism's service crashed?
   - Talos doesn't expose KubePrism as a service via `talosctl service` — it's internal to apid
   - Check `talosctl --nodes <ip> logs apid` for KubePrism errors
   - `talosctl --nodes <ip> dmesg | grep -i kubeprism`
3. Did KubePrism fail to start because of dependency on `trustd` / `apid` cert chain?
   - Search results suggested: "apid needs trustd to get its certificate signed, but with certain configurations there is no Talos control plane so there is no trustd, causing KubePrism to never start"
4. Is there a network policy or eBPF program blocking 7445?
5. Is `cluster.controlPlane.localAPIServerPort` actually 7445? Could be different.

## Fix paths (try in order)

1. `talosctl --nodes <ip> service apid restart` (cheap to try)
2. Re-apply machineconfig (`talosctl apply-config`) — sometimes forces re-init
3. Open issue upstream with our reproduction
4. Worst case: enable `cluster.allowSchedulingOnControlPlanes: true` + `proxy: { disabled: true }` and configure Cilium to point at the Talos VIP if we add one

## Acceptance

- KubePrism actually listens on 7445 on all 5 nodes (verifiable via `/proc/net/tcp` or curl from a host-net pod)
- Cilium safely switched to `k8sServiceHost: localhost`, `k8sServicePort: 7445` (canary on 1 worker first)
- All Cilium agents come up ready
- Document the fix in the post-mortem
acceptance_criteria:
- KubePrism listens on 7445 on every node (verified live)
- Cilium successfully switched to localhost:7445 (canary first, then all)
- All Cilium agents ready
- Root cause documented in post-mortem

title: Runbook: post network maintenance (router/switch reboot) cluster health checklist
description:
## Why

On 2026-05-27 the user restarted the router/switches earlier in the day. That brief network partition put cilium agents on talos02-gpu and talos03 into a death loop they couldn't recover from. The cluster then degraded silently for hours until plex broke.

If we'd known to check, a 30-second `kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=cilium` immediately after the maintenance would have caught it.

## Document to add

`docs/05-runbooks/post-network-maintenance.md`:

```markdown
# Post Network Maintenance Checklist

After restarting the router, switches, or any networking gear that the cluster depends on, run these checks IMMEDIATELY. Brief network partitions are the #1 trigger for Cilium agent death spirals on Talos.

## Quick Check (60 seconds)

\`\`\`bash
# 1. All cilium agents healthy?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=cilium -o wide
# Expect: all 1/1 Running, no CrashLoopBackOff, restart counts not climbing

# 2. All nodes Ready?
kubectl get nodes

# 3. Any admission webhooks broken?
scripts/safe-restart-cilium.sh --dry-run   # the pre-flight does exactly this check

# 4. Quick canary test
kubectl run net-check --rm -it --restart=Never --image=busybox -- nslookup kubernetes.default
\`\`\`

## If Cilium Agents Are CrashLoopBackOff

\`\`\`bash
# Step 1: kick the failing pod (control plane MUST be healthy for this to work)
kubectl delete pod -n kube-system <bad-cilium-pod>

# Step 2: wait 60s, recheck. If still failing, reboot the node:
talosctl --talosconfig configs/talosconfig --nodes <node-ip> reboot --mode=force --wait

# Step 3: if control plane is unreachable too, see:
#   docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-21-cilium-cascading-meltdown.md
#   docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-27-network-maintenance-cilium-meltdown.md (TODO file this incident)
\`\`\`

## Pre-Maintenance (optional but recommended)

If you know ahead of time you're rebooting network gear:
- `task k8s:dump-state` (snapshot current state for comparison)
- Note current cilium restart counts so you can spot increases post-maintenance
```

## Also file the new post-mortem doc

`docs/06-troubleshooting/2026-05-27-network-maintenance-cilium-meltdown.md` — same template as the 2026-05-21 doc, documenting:
- Trigger: router/switch reboot
- Cascade: cilium startup loop on talos02-gpu/talos03 → operators on those nodes lose apiserver → webhooks broken → cluster-wide admission stall → control plane eventually went dark
- Recovery: force-reboot talos00, kick stuck cilium pods, observe operators recover
- Why prior fix wasn't enough: bumped liveness but startup probe was the killer

## Acceptance

- New runbook in docs/05-runbooks/
- New post-mortem in docs/06-troubleshooting/
- Linked from QUICKSTART.md or top-level README under "When things break"
acceptance_criteria:
- post-network-maintenance.md runbook exists and is linked from QUICKSTART/top README
- 2026-05-27 post-mortem doc filed alongside the 2026-05-21 one

title: Bump Cilium startupProbe.failureThreshold (current loops kill agent before /healthz binds)
description:
## Why

The 2026-05-22 fix (commit a29010d) bumped Cilium's **liveness** probe failureThreshold from 10 → 30 (15min grace). That helps once an agent is *running*. But on 2026-05-27 we still saw two cilium pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff with **906 startup probe failures** each.

The startup probe is what gates "agent is ready to serve liveness checks." When the agent restarts after a network blip, the startup probe runs **before** the agent's `/healthz` endpoint is bound. If the startup probe fails too many times, kubelet kills the pod → backs off → tries again → fails again → infinite loop. The agent never gets a chance to fully initialize.

Sample from `kubectl describe pod cilium-m8v8k`:
```
Warning  Unhealthy  19h (x906 over 5d12h)  kubelet  spec.containers{cilium-agent}: Startup probe failed: Get "http://127.0.0.1:9879/healthz": dial tcp 127.0.0.1:9879: connect: connection refused
Warning  BackOff    19h (x500 over 5d12h)  kubelet  spec.containers{cilium-agent}: Back-off restarting failed container cilium-agent in pod cilium-m8v8k_kube-system
```

## Fix

In `configs/cilium-values.yaml`:

```yaml
startupProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /healthz
    port: 9879
    scheme: HTTP
    httpHeaders:
      - name: brief
        value: "true"
  failureThreshold: 105   # current default is 105 (15.75 min @ 10s period)... actually verify current
  periodSeconds: 10
  successThreshold: 1
  timeoutSeconds: 5
```

But first **verify what the current live setting is**:
```
kubectl get ds -n kube-system cilium -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[?(@.name=="cilium-agent")].startupProbe}'
```

If it's already at the Cilium default of `failureThreshold: 105`, then the issue isn't probe tolerance — it's something else (e.g. apiserver unreachable from the pod). In that case, the fix is to make the agent more resilient to apiserver hiccups during init OR add a sidecar that delays the probe.

## Notes

- Don't blindly bump — verify root cause first. If apiserver was genuinely unreachable for 5 days, no probe tolerance bump will help.
- Related: cilium v1.16.6 is what we run. Check upgrade path to v1.16.x latest (NOT 1.18.8 — see cilium#45208 regression).

## Acceptance

- Current startup probe values documented
- If too aggressive, bumped with reasoning
- Synthetic test: simulate network partition (drop apiserver traffic for 90s), confirm cilium pod survives
acceptance_criteria:
- Current startup probe config documented
- Investigation determines whether probe tuning is the right fix or something else
- If a tuning change is made, recovery from a simulated 90s network partition verified

title: Audit alert coverage: ensure DiskSpaceLow + 5-alert baseline are firing
description:
Confirm we have working alerts for the 6 universally-recommended categories. kube-prometheus-stack ships defaults but we never explicitly audited what's enabled.

The 6 to verify:
1. ServiceDown (per-app or per-namespace) — does node-exporter's `up == 0` cover what we need?
2. HighErrorRate (5xx rate from HTTP-serving apps)
3. HighLatency (P95)
4. HighCPUUsage (node-level — kube-prometheus-stack default likely covers)
5. HighMemoryUsage (node-level — kube-prometheus-stack default likely covers)
6. DiskSpaceLow — **CRITICAL post-UPS-2026-05-09**:
   - Node-level disk (/var/lib/etcd, /)
   - Each PV/PVC backed by local-path (hostPath on the node)
   - MinIO buckets capacity

For #6: `node_filesystem_avail_bytes / node_filesystem_size_bytes < 0.10 for 10m` on critical mountpoints. Check if kube-prometheus-stack's `NodeFilesystemAlmostOutOfSpace` is active and routing correctly.

title: Route desec-ddns through ProtonVPN gluetun
description:
Currently desec-ddns publishes the home WAN IP (108.64.138.156) to kelsboi.dedyn.io. User wants the site to be seen as hosted through the VPN router for kelsboi.dedyn.io traffic.

## Approach options

### Option A: ProtonVPN port-forward (preferred)
- Add gluetun sidecar to desec-ddns pod (or move desec-ddns container into the existing vpn-gateway/gluetun pod as another sidecar)
- ddns-updater queries ipify.org through the tunnel → gets the ProtonVPN exit IP
- Publishes that IP to deSEC for kelsboi.dedyn.io
- ProtonVPN port-forwarding allocates a public port pointing back into gluetun
- Need to capture the rotating port via gluetun control API at http://localhost:8000/v1/openvpn/portforwarded
- Inbound flow: deSEC A record (VPN IP) → ProtonVPN port-forwarded port → gluetun → cluster

## Things to figure out
1. **Port forwarding rotation** — ProtonVPN rotates the forwarded port periodically. Need either:
   - A sidecar that reads gluetun's control API and re-publishes the port via SRV record (complex)
   - Or assume the same port + accept that connectivity is best-effort
2. **TLS / cert issuance** — Let's Encrypt HTTP-01 won't work over an arbitrary port. cert-manager already uses Cloudflare DNS-01 for the knowledgedump.space cert. Need to ensure kelsboi.dedyn.io certs use deSEC DNS-01 (deSEC supports it).
3. **Server selection** — only certain ProtonVPN servers (CH/IS/SE/RO) support port-forwarding
4. **Routing inbound traffic** — gluetun's iptables need to forward the inbound port to a Service IP within the cluster (Traefik's :443?)
5. **Testing** — verify with curl --resolve that the VPN IP path works

## Refs
- Existing gluetun setup: infrastructure/base/vpn-gateway/deployment.yaml (vpn-rotator)
- desec-ddns pod: external-dns/desec-ddns (deployed by talos-private/external-dns-desec/ddns.yaml)
- ProtonVPN port forwarding gluetun docs: https://github.com/qdm12/gluetun-wiki/blob/main/setup/providers/protonvpn.md

## Out of scope (not for this ticket)
- Cloudflare external-dns for knowledgedump.space (separate, already enabled this session)
- Migrating qbittorrent / arr-stack to use the same VPN routing path

Discovered: 2026-05-10
notes:
Phase 1 complete (2026-05-10):
- desec-ddns now runs gluetun sidecar (qmcgaw/gluetun:v3.41.1) routing IP discovery through ProtonVPN Netherlands tunnel
- Published IP changed from home WAN (108.64.138.156) → ProtonVPN exit (212.92.104.231 / NForce Entertainment B.V., Breda, NL)
- Port forwarding confirmed working: gluetun /v1/openvpn/portforwarded returned port 45419
- protonvpn-credentials ESO replicated into external-dns namespace
- external-dns namespace bumped to PSA enforce=privileged (gluetun needs NET_ADMIN)
- ddns-updater health server moved off 127.0.0.1:9999 to avoid collision with gluetun

Commits:
- talos-private: 1e4bd94 (sidecar), 0dad8cc (port fix)
- talos-homelab: be9a42b (PSA label)

Phase 2 (still TODO — keep ticket open):
- Forward inbound traffic from gluetun's allocated public port → cluster ingress (Traefik)
- TLS for kelsboi.dedyn.io services (cert-manager DNS-01 via deSEC)
- Handle ProtonVPN port rotation (read /v1/openvpn/portforwarded periodically and either republish via SRV record or pin per-server)
- E2E test: curl --resolve <service>.kelsboi.dedyn.io:<port>:212.92.104.231

title: knowledge-graph-api crashes with empty Neo4j schema
description:
Pod catalyst-data/knowledge-graph-api in CrashLoopBackOff (127 restarts/10h). Logs show:

  [graphql-api] Connecting to Neo4j at bolt://neo4j.catalyst-data.svc.cluster.local:7687 ...
  [graphql-api] Neo4j connectivity OK.
  [graphql-api] Introspecting Neo4j schema ...
  [graphql-api] Introspection complete (39 chars of SDL).
  [graphql-api] Startup failed: Error: Type Query must define one or more fields.

Root cause: chicken-and-egg — the GraphQL API uses @neo4j/graphql introspection to auto-generate its schema from existing nodes/relationships. With an empty Neo4j (post UPS-2026-05-09 PV recovery), no schema is generated, Apollo Server refuses to start.

Fix options (all in external catalyst-data repo, github.com:TheBranchDriftCatalyst/catalyst-data):
1. Make the API tolerant of empty schemas (define a placeholder Type Query before introspection)
2. Pre-populate Neo4j with a minimal seed node so introspection produces a valid schema
3. Add an init container that runs a Cypher seed script before the API starts

Image: ghcr.io/thebranchdriftcatalyst/knowledge-graph-api:latest
Discovered: 2026-05-10

title: Restore synology-666 volume mounts for tdarr-server/worker
description:
When fixing arr-stack-private ArgoCD sync, tdarr-patches/kustomization.yaml was changed from resources: to patches: (UPS-2026-05-09 recovery). The strategic merge patches now silently drop because the target resources (tdarr-server Deployment, tdarr-worker DaemonSet) live in the public arr-stack repo, not this kustomization tree.

Result: synology-666 volumeMount/volume additions are NO LONGER reaching the cluster. Tdarr can no longer transcode private media on the synology /666 share.

Fix options:
1. Include the upstream arr-stack base as remote kustomize resource so patches have targets
2. Move synology-666 volume additions to the public arr-stack repo (cleaner — no cross-repo dependency)
3. Use ArgoCD spec.source.kustomize.patches field so ArgoCD applies the patches via SSA against in-cluster objects

Recommend option 2 (move to public repo) for simplicity.

Repo: github.com:TheBranchDriftCatalyst/private at tdarr-patches/
notes:
**VERIFIED 2026-05-22**: Public base path and container indexes corrected.

## Verified facts from public talos-homelab repo

- **Path**: `applications/tdarr/base/` (NOT `applications/arr-stack/base/tdarr/`)
- **Namespace**: `tdarr`
- **Server**: Deployment `tdarr-server`, containers[]:
  - `[0]` flow-importer (init-style, do NOT patch)
  - `[1]` **tdarr** ← patch volumeMounts here
- **Worker**: DaemonSet `tdarr-worker`, containers[]:
  - `[0]` **tdarr-node** ← patch volumeMounts here
- Existing volumes in both already include `synology-movies` + `synology-tv`,
  synology-666 will be a 3rd alongside them.

## Corrected kustomization for talos-private/tdarr-patches/

```yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
namespace: tdarr   # NOTE: not "media"

resources:
  # Pull public tdarr base. Pin to a specific commit for stability instead
  # of ?ref=main — upgrade deliberately when public base changes.
  - github.com/TheBranchDriftCatalyst/talos-homelab//applications/tdarr/base?ref=<commit-or-tag>
  # If synology-666 PVC isn't already present in cluster, define here:
  # - pvc-synology-666.yaml

patches:
  # tdarr-server (Deployment): add synology-666 mount to containers[1]=tdarr
  - target:
      kind: Deployment
      name: tdarr-server
    patch: |
      - op: add
        path: /spec/template/spec/containers/1/volumeMounts/-
        value:
          name: synology-666
          mountPath: /mnt/synology-666
      - op: add
        path: /spec/template/spec/volumes/-
        value:
          name: synology-666
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: synology-666

  # tdarr-worker (DaemonSet): add synology-666 mount to containers[0]=tdarr-node
  - target:
      kind: DaemonSet
      name: tdarr-worker
    patch: |
      - op: add
        path: /spec/template/spec/containers/0/volumeMounts/-
        value:
          name: synology-666
          mountPath: /mnt/synology-666
      - op: add
        path: /spec/template/spec/volumes/-
        value:
          name: synology-666
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: synology-666
```

## Validate post-deploy

```bash
kubectl get deploy -n tdarr tdarr-server -o yaml | \
  yq '.spec.template.spec.containers[1].volumeMounts'
# Expect: config, server, logs, flows, synology-movies, synology-tv, synology-666

kubectl get ds -n tdarr tdarr-worker -o yaml | \
  yq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].volumeMounts'
# Expect: node-info, transcode-cache, dri, synology-movies, synology-tv, synology-666

# Inside the pods, ls /mnt/synology-666 should show the share
kubectl exec -n tdarr deploy/tdarr-server -c tdarr -- ls /mnt/synology-666 | head -5
```

title: Fix qbittorrent themepark mod restart loop
description:

title: Migrate scratch-mongodb from NFS to local-path SC
description:
MongoDB's WiredTiger storage engine corrupts on NFS during unclean shutdowns (UPS-2026-05-09 incident).

Root cause:
- Single-replica StatefulSet means no peer to recover from — only option after corruption is full data wipe
- mongo on NFS is generally discouraged due to fsync/locking semantics issues
- Combined with NFS provisioner archiveOnDelete bug (separate ticket), recovery is painful

Recommendations (pick one):
A) Switch StatefulSet to local-path SC (simplest)
B) Add 2nd replica for HA so peer can resync after corruption
C) Both A and B for max safety

Tradeoff for option A:
- local-path means data lives on a single node
- Lose the node = lose the data
- But this IS the 'scratch' namespace, so acceptable

File location: applications/scratch/.../mongodb (find it via grep for the StatefulSet)

Discovered: 2026-05-09 (UPS recovery incident)

title: NFS provisioner archiveOnDelete=true not working — old data persists across PVC recreate
description:
HelmRelease at infrastructure/base/storage/nfs-provisioner/helmrelease.yaml sets archiveOnDelete: true, but during UPS-2026-05-09 recovery, deleting a PVC and recreating it left ALL old data in place.

Verified via direct NFS mount: 535MB of old data persisted after PVC delete/recreate.

Impact:
- This silently caused mongodb to repeatedly hit WT_PANIC on the corrupted data even after the PVC was 'wiped'
- Operators have a false sense that PVC deletion = data wipe

Workaround used during recovery:
- Manually wipe NFS subdirectory contents via debug pod with: find . -mindepth 1 -delete

Investigation needed:
1. Is archiveOnDelete actually wired up correctly in the helm chart?
2. Compare deployed helm chart values vs. what's in helmrelease.yaml
3. Consider switching to 'archive' strategy with cleanup CronJob instead
4. Document expected behavior so we don't get bit again

Discovered: 2026-05-09 (UPS recovery incident)

During the UPS-2026-05-09 cluster recovery, this migration was marked as --applied to unblock the deployment, so the FK constraint and trigger are NOT actually enforced.

title: Velero PrometheusRule: alert on missing PodVolumeBackups in fs-backup runs
description:
Sister to TALOS-a8g's etcd-backup PrometheusRule. The 2026-05-09 audit revealed Velero ran 'successfully' but produced zero PodVolumeBackup CRs because pods were excluded. A success-by-itemcount alert would have missed this.

Add alerts:
- VeleroBackupFailed: kube_job-equivalent for velero Backup CRs in failed/error phase
- VeleroFsBackupZero: alert when a Backup completed but produced 0 PodVolumeBackup CRs while annotated pods existed in the included namespaces (silent-success detector)
- VeleroScheduleNotRunning: no successful Backup in 2x schedule interval

This is the failure mode that would have caught the pods-excluded bug before disaster.
close_reason: Added velero-backup-alerts PrometheusRule (commit f21ac1a): VeleroBackupFailed, VeleroBackupZeroItems (silent-success detector), VeleroScheduleNotRunning, VeleroDown. Wired into infrastructure/base/backup/kustomization.yaml so Flux picks it up. Catches exactly the failure mode that contributed to UPS-2026-05-09 unrecoverability.

title: Homepage widget credentials — create 1Password items + regenerate tokens
description:
Several homepage widgets show "API Error Information" because the ExternalSecrets reference 1Password items that don't exist yet. The plumbing is in place (ExternalSecrets created in `52c52ac`, env vars on the homepage pod with `optional: true`) — just need the 1Password items created.

## Items to create in 1Password (vault: catalyst-eso)

### 1. qBittorrent web UI credentials
- **1Password item name:** `qbittorrent-webui`
- **Fields:** `username` = `panda`, `password` = `turbopookipanda`
- **Homepage env vars:** `HOMEPAGE_VAR_QBITTORRENT_USER`, `HOMEPAGE_VAR_QBITTORRENT_PASS`
- **ExternalSecret:** `applications/arr-stack/base/homepage/qbittorrent-externalsecret.yaml`

### 2. Tautulli API key
- **1Password item name:** `tautulli-api-key`
- **Fields:** `password` = (get from Tautulli → Settings → Web Interface → API key)
- **Homepage env var:** `HOMEPAGE_VAR_TAUTULLI_KEY`
- **ExternalSecret:** `applications/arr-stack/base/homepage/tautulli-externalsecret.yaml`

### 3. Home Assistant long-lived access token
- **1Password item name:** `homeassistant-token`
- **Fields:** `password` = (generate in HA → Settings → User → Security → Long-Lived Access Tokens → Create Token)
- **Homepage env var:** `HOMEPAGE_VAR_HOMEASSISTANT_KEY`
- **ExternalSecret:** `applications/arr-stack/base/homepage/homeassistant-externalsecret.yaml`

### 4. Authentik API token (regenerate)
- **1Password item name:** `authentik-api-token` (already exists but token returns 403)
- **Action:** Generate new API token in Authentik Admin → Directory → Tokens & App passwords (Intent: API Token). Grant permissions: "Can view User" (authentik Core), "Can view Event" (authentik Events). Update the 1Password item with the new token.
- **Homepage env var:** `HOMEPAGE_VAR_AUTHENTIK_KEY`

## After creating items
ESO refreshes hourly. To speed it up:
```bash
kubectl annotate externalsecret -n media homepage-qbittorrent-credentials force-sync=$(date +%s) --overwrite
kubectl annotate externalsecret -n media homepage-tautulli-credentials force-sync=$(date +%s) --overwrite
kubectl annotate externalsecret -n media homepage-homeassistant-credentials force-sync=$(date +%s) --overwrite
kubectl annotate externalsecret -n media homepage-authentik-credentials force-sync=$(date +%s) --overwrite
kubectl delete pod -n media -l app=homepage
```

## Also investigate
- **Radarr** widget shows all dashes — may be a transient issue or the API key might need rotation. Check `kubectl logs -n media deploy/homepage | grep -i radarr` after other widgets are fixed.
- **MinIO** siteMonitor shows Offline — the `/minio/health/live` endpoint may require auth. Try changing to `/minio/health/cluster` or removing siteMonitor.
- **Tdarr** siteMonitor shows Offline despite correct URL — may be intermittent slow responses exceeding homepage's timeout.

title: Migrate Home Assistant recorder off NFS-backed SQLite
description:
Home Assistant's `/config` volume is NFS-backed (`fatboy-nfs-appdata`, PVC `homeassistant-config-nfs`). The SQLite recorder database (`home-assistant_v2.db`) on NFS causes:

1. **SIGBUS crashes**: `Fatal Python error: Bus error` during SQLAlchemy recorder commits — SQLite's mmap + NFS coherency is a known-bad combo
2. **Liveness probe kills**: NFS latency spikes stall the asyncio event loop → `GET /` probe times out → kubelet kills pod (exit 137)
3. **69 restarts** accumulated, most recent 2026-04-08

A probe relaxation was applied as a bandaid (`66be6c8` — loosened liveness from ~150s to ~600s kill window), but the fundamental issue remains: SQLite on NFS is unsafe.

Options (pick one):
A) **Migrate recorder to external DB**: Add `recorder.db_url: postgresql://...` to HA's `configuration.yaml`, pointing at a dedicated postgres instance (could reuse cloudnative-pg in the `databases` namespace). This is the HA-recommended approach for anything beyond a single SD card.
B) **Move /config to local-path PVC**: Preserves SQLite but puts it on local disk. Downside: node-pinned, need Velero backups for persistence across node failures.
C) **Hybrid**: local-path PVC for the SQLite DB file only (via a separate volume mount at `/config/.storage/`), keep the rest of /config on NFS for cross-node access.

Also noted:
- HA container has **no memory limit** (only request 512Mi, currently using ~450Mi). Consider adding `limits.memory: 2Gi` to prevent unbounded growth.
- HA logs to `/config/home-assistant.log` on disk, not stdout. Add `HASS_LOG_TO_STDOUT=1` for better k8s-native log access.

Deployment file: `applications/home-automation/base/homeassistant/deployment.yaml`
PVC file: `applications/home-automation/base/homeassistant/pvc.yaml`
close_reason: Deployed dedicated postgres:15-alpine in home-automation namespace (postgres.yaml). Configured HA recorder to use postgresql:// via configmap + init-container patch. Verified: 13 recorder tables created in postgres, HA serving web UI, 0 restarts. Tightened liveness probe timings now that SQLite-on-NFS root cause is eliminated. Old SQLite DB remains on NFS as backup. Commit 7f44c81.

title: Apply otel-operator leaderElection.enabled=false to stop restart flapping
description:
The `monitoring/opentelemetry-operator` manager container has accumulated 2070+ restarts because it exits on leader election lease loss (controller-runtime default behavior). Any brief API server network blip (~20s) causes the lease renewal to time out → exit code 1 → kubelet restart. ~34 flaps/day historically.

Root cause confirmed from previous-container logs showing:
```
failed to renew lease monitoring/9f7554c3.opentelemetry.io: timed out waiting for the condition
"problem running manager", "error": "leader election lost"
```

NOT OOM (exit code 1, not OOMKilled). Resources are chart defaults: requests 100m/64Mi, limits 100m/128Mi — adequate.

Since this is a single-replica deployment with no HA coordination needed, disabling leader election eliminates the failure path entirely.

Fix: edit `infrastructure/base/monitoring/v2-otel/operators/otel-operator/helmrelease.yaml`, add under `values`:
```yaml
manager:
  leaderElection:
    enabled: false
```

This will trigger a pod rollout. Apply during a quiet period since the pod is currently stable (last restart 2026-04-08). Upstream reference: open-telemetry/opentelemetry-operator#2460.

Current resources (chart defaults):
- manager: requests cpu=100m mem=64Mi, limits cpu=100m mem=128Mi
- kube-rbac-proxy: requests cpu=5m mem=64Mi, limits cpu=500m mem=128Mi
close_reason: Applied leaderElection.enabled=false in helmrelease.yaml. Pod rolled out with 0 restarts (5 transient restarts during rollout due to ServiceMonitor conflict, resolved by deleting stale ServiceMonitor). New pod running 2/2 on talos06.
